title: The Story Of Terracotta In Sri Lankan Architecture
excerpt: From the ancient palaces of Anuradhapura to the colonial streets of Galle, terracotta has kept Sri Lankan homes cool and dry for over two thousand years. Journey through the history of our island's most iconic, breathable material.

content.value: When you travel across Sri Lanka, certain sights stay with you forever. The deep green of the tea estates, the blue of the southern ocean, and the warm, earthy red of clay-tiled roofs peeking through the coconut palms. Terracotta, which literally translates to baked earth, is woven directly into the fabric of our island's history.

Sri Lanka has a long tradition of clay roofing, with evidence of its use dating back to ancient times. For generations, our builders, kings, and artisans have relied on this humble material to create spaces that are beautiful, sustainable, and suited to the tropical climate.
Ancient Beginnings In Anuradhapura

The history of clay tiles in Sri Lanka goes back to at least the third century BC. When Buddhism arrived on the island, it brought a wave of architectural innovation. Traditional clay tile craftsmanship became an important part of the island’s architectural heritage, led by the
ulu waduwa
, the traditional tile maker. These artisans were highly respected members of society, tasked with creating roofs for royal palaces, monasteries, and grand state buildings.
The earliest clay tiles were flat and known as
pethi ulu

, or Kandyan tiles. These flat tiles were incredibly hardy. In fact, archaeological excavations in our ancient capitals have unearthed remarkably preserved tile fragments that have survived the elements for centuries. The ancient tile makers were masters of their craft, using natural glazes that allowed heavy monsoon rains to slide right off the clay, protecting the timber structures below.
The Evolution Of Sinhala Ulu
If you have ever visited Galle Fort or walked through older neighborhoods in Colombo, you have likely seen the half-round, barrel-shaped tiles that cover colonial-era buildings.
Portuguese and Dutch colonial influence helped popularize the half-round
Sinhala ulu
tile. Over time, local craftsmen fully integrated this design into the island's traditional building styles.

This design was highly effective for our climate. The overlapping curves of the tiles created natural channels for heavy rainwater to drain away quickly, while the air pockets underneath acted as natural insulation against the intense midday sun. It represents a beautiful blend of colonial influence and local clay craftsmanship.
A Material That Understands The Tropics
Our ancestors did not use terracotta just because it was available; they used it because it worked. They understood that a tropical home needs to breathe.

Many designers appreciate clay as a seemingly living, breathing material. Unlike some modern concrete or metal roofing options, which can trap heat inside tropical homes, clay has microscopic pores that allow air to circulate. During the hot dry season, a terracotta roof or wall helps keep indoor temperatures comfortable. During the monsoon, it helps regulate the dampness in the air, preventing that musty, humid feeling.
Bringing The Heritage Into The Modern Era

As modern construction swept through Sri Lanka, materials like concrete and plastic began to push traditional clay aside. Many contemporary spaces lost their natural ventilation, and our architectural connection to the earth began to fade.
At Terra by Shavindi, we believe that the future of Sri Lankan architecture lies in its past.

We are on a mission to bring terracotta back into modern, minimal, and boho spaces. Our designs respect the ancient wisdom of traditional clay craft while adapting the material for contemporary homes. Whether it is a feature wall of breathable clay tiles, a hand-formed planter, or custom architectural elements, we source our clay locally and work with Sri Lankan artisans to keep this beautiful heritage alive.
The Timeless Appeal Of Baked Earth

There is a soul in terracotta that synthetic materials simply cannot replicate. It is the color of our soil, shaped by local hands, and baked by fire. As it ages, it does not decay; it develops a rich, unique patina that tells the story of the seasons it has weathered.

Choosing terracotta is not just a design choice. It is a way to honor our architectural heritage, support local craftsmanship, and build a home that is in harmony with the tropical island we call home.
Explore the architectural and homeware collections at Terra by Shavindi, and bring a piece of Sri Lankan history into your living space.
